title: Case Discussion: Scope, Evidence Tiers, and Ethical Boundaries
clinicalScenario: A 40-year-old asks you to confirm they have androgenetic alopecia in one visit and requests exosome injections after reading a clinic advert promising regrowth without drugs. They feel distressed and want a procedure within two weeks. They have no scalp pain or rash. They ask you to rank surgical versus exosome options as the only choices.
discussionPrompts: How do you translate their request into a clinical process category before discussing any procedure (Volume 1 Module 1)?
Using the manual four-tier model, how do you position exosome therapy versus established medical options without overclaiming?
What ethical and consent issues arise when psychological distress meets commercial urgency, and what documentation supports safe practice?
moderatorNotes: Faculty: ensure learners separate diagnosis from marketing, cite Tier 1–4 language, and refuse premature irreversible planning. Reference manual sections 1.1, 1.4, and 1.5.
redFlags: Commercial urgency driving procedure within two weeks without diagnosis
Request for Tier 4 intervention framed as certainty
Psychological distress without documented clinical assessment

title: Case Discussion: Cycle Biology versus Scarring Risk
clinicalScenario: A 35-year-old reports 4 months of increasing hair fall after stopping oral contraception; she also notes tight ponytails most days and occasional vertex tenderness. On questioning she has not examined her scalp closely. She believes it is only telogen effluvium from hormones.
discussionPrompts: How does cycle biology support or challenge a pure telogen effluvium hypothesis here?
Which elements in history should trigger careful examination for traction or early cicatricial disease?
How does bulge/stem-niche biology inform why you might expedite assessment if follicular openings appear diminished?
moderatorNotes: Faculty: use manual Module 2 sections 2.5 and 2.3; link traction and symptoms to examination urgency; avoid confirming TE without exam.
redFlags: Risk of traction alopecia or early cicatricial change until examination excludes
Premature closure on telogen effluvium without scalp exam

title: Case Discussion: Molecular Framing and Adjunctive Therapies
clinicalScenario: A 50-year-old man with long-standing vertex thinning asks to stop finasteride because of anxiety online and replace it with monthly PRP only. He has no contraindications documented and stable cardiac history. He wants a guarantee of density recovery.
discussionPrompts: How does the manual treatment-logic section distinguish core anti-androgen therapy from adjunctive regenerative options?
What should you explain about evidence heterogeneity for PRP (Tier 2–3) versus finasteride (Tier 1 in appropriate male AGA)?
How do you document shared decision-making when a patient requests discontinuation of a Tier 1 therapy?
moderatorNotes: Faculty: stress no guarantees, maintenance expectations, and medico-legal documentation; reference Module 3 sections 3.5 and 3.8.
redFlags: Abandoning Tier 1 therapy for adjunct-only plan without clinical review
Misrepresentation of PRP evidence as uniform or curative

title: Case Discussion: Structured History, Red Flags, and Documentation
clinicalScenario: A 24-year-old woman presents with diffuse shedding for 8 weeks and mild widening of her part. She reports irregular periods, new jawline acne, and 6 kg weight gain over a year. She denies scalp pain but notes increased dandruff. She is not pregnant. She requests hair transplant referral immediately.
discussionPrompts: Classify her complaint(s) and construct a differential branch before any surgical discussion.
What minimum endocrine and reproductive history is still required, and which investigations might you consider before referral (local protocol dependent)?
Which red-flag features would change urgency if she reported scalp burning, pustules, or rapid patchy loss—and how would you document uncertainty after the first consult?
moderatorNotes: Faculty: drill manual Module 4 sections 4.1–4.6 and 4.9; block premature transplant discussion; emphasise hyperandrogenism pathway and examination-first discipline.
redFlags: Irregular menses with acne and weight gain suggesting hyperandrogenism
Premature surgical referral before endocrine and examination workup
Diffuse shedding plus pattern change needing structured differentials
